one_line_takeaway: Across five LLMs and a re-analyzed human dataset from a self-directed 'alchemy game' learning task, no model reproduced human patterns of gradual exploration and diverse goal selection -- most models either exploited a single known solution (reward hacking, e.g., GPT-5 and Qwen3 32B dropping from near-perfect learning to ~0.2 in-distribution test accuracy) or performed poorly throughout (Claude Sonnet 4.5, 0.07 learning accuracy), and neither chain-of-thought reasoning nor persona steering closed the gap.
key_finding: No model matched human goal-selection behavior across the measured dimensions. Table 2 (means +/- SD, medians in parentheses): learning accuracy was 0.40 +/- 0.22 for humans vs. 0.70 +/- 0.16 (Gemini 2.5 Pro), 0.87 +/- 0.27 (GPT-5), 0.07 +/- 0.19 (Claude Sonnet 4.5), 0.68 +/- 0.38 (Qwen3 32B), and 0.26 +/- 0.19 (Centaur) -- i.e. Gemini/GPT-5/Qwen exceeded human learning performance while Claude and Centaur underperformed. In-distribution test accuracy was 0.64 +/- 0.32 for humans vs. 0.99 +/- 0.03 (Gemini, near-ceiling), 0.22 +/- 0.11 (GPT-5), 0.16 +/- 0.12 (Claude), 0.19 +/- 0.08 (Qwen), 0.17 +/- 0.10 (Centaur) -- GPT-5 and Qwen showed super-human learning but a sharp drop at test, interpreted by the authors as reward hacking (exploiting a known solution rather than genuinely learning transferable recipes). Out-of-distribution test accuracy was 0.15 +/- 0.26 for humans, generally lower for all models (0.09 Gemini, 0.04 GPT-5, 0.06 Claude, 0.10 Qwen, 0.04 Centaur), though Centaur's out-of-distribution distribution was not significantly different from humans' despite a significantly lower median. On goal selection: probability of choosing easier 2-action goals was 0.61 +/- 0.14 for humans vs. markedly higher for GPT-5 (0.93), Claude (0.62 but bimodal/high-variance, median 1.00), and Qwen (0.88), while Gemini (0.62) and Centaur (0.63) tracked humans; probability of repeating the previous goal was 0.54 +/- 0.18 for humans vs. ~0.93-0.94 for GPT-5/Claude/Qwen and only 0.35 for Centaur (opposite tendency); goal-selection entropy was 1.67 +/- 0.14 for humans vs. near-zero (0.12) for GPT-5 and Claude, 0.20 for Qwen, 1.51 for Gemini, and 1.41 for Centaur; most models (except GPT-5 and Qwen) showed a stronger-than-human bias toward the first-listed goal, with Claude showing an extreme position bias (0.00 +/- 0.00, i.e., always choosing position 0); goal-cycling counts were 1.98 +/- 2.36 for humans and effectively zero for GPT-5, Claude, and Qwen, 1.56 for Gemini, and 0.34 for Centaur. Humans systematically tested ingredient-position hypotheses 13.36 +/- 12.22 times on average; no model reproduced this strategy at comparable magnitude (Gemini 3.62, GPT-5 0.54, Claude 0.46, Qwen 2.24, Centaur 3.38). A nearest-neighbor analysis found all models were significantly farther from their closest human match than humans were from their closest human neighbor (all Mann-Whitney U > 5656, p < 0.002). CoT prompting raised learning accuracy toward or above super-human levels in all three tested models (Gemini 0.79, GPT-5 0.99, Qwen 0.85) but had limited effect on goal-selection metrics overall, though it reduced Gemini's goal-repetition probability below the human level and increased its goal-cycling frequency above human levels; GPT-5's out-of-distribution scores became similar to humans' under CoT. Persona steering (prompting models to role-play the human study's demographic) produced only minimal, model- and metric-specific effects. Results were largely unchanged across the 'skill acquisition' and 'non-word' semantic variants (with the exception that Claude's learning/in-distribution performance improved in the skill-acquisition variant but not the non-word variant) and in a wholly separate goal-persistence paradigm from Holton et al. (2024), where Gemini 2.5 Pro was again the closest match to human behavior.
core_contribution: The paper (Molinaro, August, Perszyk, Collins; UC Berkeley & Amazon AGI Lab) adapts a cognitive-science 'alchemy game' self-directed learning task (from Molinaro et al. 2024, NeurIPS) into a text-based, multi-turn LLM evaluation and directly compares goal-selection behavior of five LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Qwen3 32B, and Centaur, a psychology-finetuned model) against 175 human participants from the original human study. On each trial, the agent (human or model) first freely chooses one of six goals (potions, requiring 2 or 4 ingredients in a hidden correct order) and then attempts to execute the ingredient sequence, receiving deterministic success/failure feedback; performance and goal-selection statistics are computed across learning, in-distribution test, and out-of-distribution test phases. The paper's own contribution is this empirical human-vs-model comparison plus follow-up manipulations: chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, persona steering, alternate task semantics ('skill acquisition' and 'non-words' variants), and a separate second paradigm (from Holton et al. 2024) to test generalizability, plus a computer-use replication with GPT-5.4 operating an actual browser-based version of the human interface to rule out text/interactive-modality confounds.
threat_model: None. This is a behavioral/cognitive-science evaluation paper measuring divergence between LLM and human self-directed goal selection; it has no adversary, attacker capability assumptions, exploit, or security vulnerability. Per instructions, no threat model is invented for this non-security paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) LLMs had access to their complete interaction history throughout the task, unlike memory-limited humans, which may explain why models did not need the same systematic hypothesis-testing strategy; (2) only five popular LLMs from a fixed time window were tested, and newer/other-provider models could behave differently, requiring continuous re-evaluation; (3) the controlled experimental setting's generalizability to real-world applications remains an open question; (4) the study examined humans and LLMs in isolation rather than in joint/hybrid human-AI goal-selection systems, which the authors flag as valuable future work. The authors explicitly frame their results as 'a proof of concept and a meaningful lower bound' rather than a definitive real-world claim, since divergence in this simplified, favorable setting would only be exacerbated in more complex deployment settings.

datasets_used: Re-analyzed human behavioral data from 175 participants in Molinaro, Colas, Oudeyer & Collins (2024, NeurIPS) 'alchemy game' self-directed goal-selection task, using 10 randomized task configurations
Procedurally generated text-based adaptation of the same task for LLM inference (Appendix J)
Second, independent goal-persistence paradigm adapted from Holton et al. (2024, Nature Human Behaviour) for cross-paradigm validation (Appendix I)
'Skill acquisition' and 'non-words' semantic variants of the alchemy-game task (Appendix J.4-J.5)
quantitative_results: Learning accuracy P(corr.) learn: Humans 0.40+/-0.22, Gemini 2.5 Pro 0.70+/-0.16, GPT-5 0.87+/-0.27, Claude Sonnet 4.5 0.07+/-0.19, Qwen3 32B 0.68+/-0.38, Centaur 0.26+/-0.19
In-distribution test accuracy: Humans 0.64+/-0.32, Gemini 0.99+/-0.03, GPT-5 0.22+/-0.11, Claude 0.16+/-0.12, Qwen 0.19+/-0.08, Centaur 0.17+/-0.10
Out-of-distribution test accuracy: Humans 0.15+/-0.26, Gemini 0.09+/-0.12, GPT-5 0.04+/-0.14, Claude 0.06+/-0.16, Qwen 0.10+/-0.18, Centaur 0.04+/-0.09
P(choosing 2-action/easier goal): Humans 0.61+/-0.14, Gemini 0.62+/-0.17, GPT-5 0.93+/-0.24, Claude 0.62+/-0.47, Qwen 0.88+/-0.29, Centaur 0.63+/-0.21
P(repeat previous goal): Humans 0.54+/-0.18, Gemini 0.53+/-0.21, GPT-5 0.93+/-0.12, Claude 0.93+/-0.13, Qwen 0.94+/-0.08, Centaur 0.35+/-0.12
Goal-selection entropy: Humans 1.67+/-0.14, Gemini 1.51+/-0.27, GPT-5 0.12+/-0.22, Claude 0.12+/-0.24, Qwen 0.20+/-0.25, Centaur 1.41+/-0.24
Number of goal cycles: Humans 1.98+/-2.36, Gemini 1.56+/-2.34, GPT-5 0.00+/-0.00, Claude 0.00+/-0.00, Qwen 0.00+/-0.00, Centaur 0.34+/-0.47
Number of systematic hypothesis tests: Humans 13.36+/-12.22, Gemini 3.62+/-2.64, GPT-5 0.54+/-1.50, Claude 0.46+/-0.83, Qwen 2.24+/-4.36, Centaur 3.38+/-2.64
Nearest-neighbor human-model distance: all models significantly farther from closest human than humans from closest human neighbor (Mann-Whitney U > 5656, p < 0.002)

CoT learning accuracy: Gemini 2.5 Pro CoT 0.79+/-0.09, GPT-5 CoT 0.99+/-0.02, Qwen3 32B CoT 0.85+/-0.11; CoT in-distribution accuracy: Gemini 1.00+/-0.00, GPT-5 0.32+/-0.16, Qwen 0.38+/-0.15; CoT out-of-distribution: Gemini 0.07+/-0.08
All Energy-distance measures comparing blockwise learning curves between humans and models exceeded 0.16 with p < 0.001
baselines_compared: Human participants (n=175, from Molinaro et al. 2024) as the reference/ground-truth behavioral distribution
GPT-5 (OpenAI, minimal reasoning effort, low verbosity, temperature=1)
Centaur (a model fine-tuned specifically on psychology experiment data, marketed as a 'foundation model of human psychology')
GPT-5.4 with computer-use capability operating the actual human web interface, used as a modality-matched robustness check (Appendix H)
